criteria: The agent has read Section 3 completely to understand all five components of the Cache-of-Thought (CoT) framework: master VLM, apprentice VLM, model multiplexer, cache with HNSW index, and retrieval module.

The agent has read Section 4.1 completely to understand CoT's formulation: the query notation (x_t, q_t), the multiplexer function J mapping queries to {f, g}, the cache L_t with bounded size L, and the retrieval mechanism R returning top-k QA pairs.

The agent has read Section 4.2 completely to understand multi-modal in-context learning, including the prompt construction format and the instruction to include reasoning steps when querying the master VLM.

The agent has read Section 4.3.1 completely to understand the dual-modality dense retrieval using CLIP image and text encoders, keyword extraction with an auxiliary LLM, and the option to use apprentice VLM responses for improved text embeddings.

The agent has read Section 4.3.2 completely to understand the multi-stage hashtag retrieval: Level 2 hashtags from CLIP text encoder on extracted keywords, K-Means clustering to form Level 1 hashtags, and two-stage retrieval (filter by Level 1, then dense top-k).

The agent has read Section 5.1 to understand dataset details: MMMU after filtering multi-image instances yields dev 146, val 857, and test 9702; VL-ICL uses TextOCR and Clevr subtasks with dev 200 and val 800.

The agent has read Table 1 to understand Qwen-7B MMMU performance under various retrieval strategies and two caching strategies (Val-as-cache/test-Dev and Dev-as-cache/test-Val) in the static setting.
The agent has read Tables 2 and 3 to understand OpenFlamingo-3B and 9B performance on MMMU and VL-ICL (CLEVR, TextOCR) under different cache sizes and n-shot configurations.

The agent has read Appendix A completely to understand all prompt templates: OpenFlamingo prompts (MMMU, CLEVR, TextOCR), Qwen MMMU prompt, GPT-4o prompts, and Llama keyword extractor prompts for MMMU and CLEVR/TextOCR.

The agent has read Figures 5, 6, and 7 to understand dynamic performance: MMMU score vs. cache configurations, apprentice VLM performance gains across initialization methods, and per-split temporal improvement.
The plan includes implementing the cache data structure with a bounded capacity L storing QA pairs (image, question, answer) indexed by an HNSW data structure for efficient ANN vector search.
The plan includes implementing the model multiplexer that routes incoming queries to the master VLM or apprentice VLM based on a configurable probability, controlling the cost-quality trade-off.
The plan includes implementing dual-modality dense retrieval using CLIP image and text encoders with averaged embeddings, supplemented by keyword extraction via an auxiliary LLM.
The plan includes implementing multi-stage hashtag retrieval with a two-level hashtag tree, K-Means clustering for Level 1 hashtags, and two-stage retrieval (Level 1 filter then top-k dense).
The plan includes loading and filtering the MMMU dataset by removing multi-image instances, producing dev (146), val (857), and test (9702) splits.
The plan includes loading the VL-ICL dataset for the TextOCR and Clevr subtasks with dev (200) and val (800) splits.
The plan includes integrating GPT-4o as the master VLM and implementing its prompt templates from Appendix A for MMMU, CLEVR, and TextOCR.
The plan includes integrating Qwen-VL-2 7B and OpenFlamingo-3B/9B as apprentice VLMs with their respective in-context learning prompt templates from Appendix A.
The plan includes implementing both static evaluation (fixed cache, apprentice-only) and dynamic evaluation (streaming data, growing cache, configurable master/apprentice ratio).
The plan includes implementing n-shot in-context learning with varying numbers of retrieved examples (0-shot, 1-shot, 2-shot) for both OpenFlamingo and Qwen experiments.

The plan includes implementing a rule-based parser to extract the final answer: letter choice for multiple-choice (MMMU) and exact match check for open responses (VL-ICL), returning None when parsing fails.
The plan includes cache initialization strategies: warm start (pre-loading QA pairs from dev or val set) and cold start (empty cache), as used in Figures 5 and 6.
The cache data structure has been implemented to store QA pairs (image, question, answer) with a configurable bounded capacity L and an HNSW index for ANN vector search.

The cache insert operation has been implemented: when a master VLM call occurs, the QA pair is stored in the cache, its dual-modality embedding is computed, and the embedding is inserted into the HNSW index.
The cache eviction policy has been implemented (LRU or LFU) to maintain bounded cache size L when new insertions exceed capacity.
Warm start initialization has been implemented to pre-populate the cache with QA pairs from an offline dataset (e.g., the dev or val set) before processing the query stream.
Cold start initialization (empty cache) has been implemented as an alternative to warm start, allowing the cache to grow organically as the master VLM processes incoming queries.

The model multiplexer has been implemented to route each incoming query to either the master VLM or the apprentice VLM according to a configurable usage probability (e.g., 10%, 30%, 50%, 70%, 90% apprentice VLM rate).

The CLIP image encoder and CLIP text encoder have been integrated to produce separate image and text embeddings, which are then averaged to form a unified dual-modality embedding vector for each QA pair.

The auxiliary LLM (Llama) keyword extractor has been implemented using the MMMU prompt from Appendix A: given a question-option context, it produces exactly 10 keywords separated by commas, excluding 'question' and 'options'.
The auxiliary LLM (Llama) keyword extractor for CLEVR and TextOCR has been implemented using the corresponding prompt from Appendix A: given a context, it produces 10 keywords excluding 'ANSWER'.

The apprentice VLM auxiliary pass for retrieval enhancement has been implemented: when a query's text is insufficiently informative, the apprentice VLM generates a response and keywords are extracted from that response to form a richer text embedding for retrieval.
The dual-modality dense retrieval has been implemented to perform top-k ANN search in the HNSW index using the averaged CLIP image and keyword-extracted text embeddings of the incoming query.
Level 2 hashtags have been implemented as CLIP text encoder embeddings applied to keywords extracted from master VLM answers: h_{2,t} = CLIP_TextEncoder(Extractor(a_t)).

K-Means clustering is triggered when |H2| > tau or cold start ends, grouping Level 2 hashtags into K clusters and computing Level 1 hashtag h_{1,k} as the mean embedding of its corresponding Level 2 hashtag children.
The Level 1 hashtag assignment for new cache entries has been implemented: each new master VLM answer is assigned to the closest Level 1 hashtag based on Euclidean distance.
The Level 1 hashtag update rule has been implemented as a weighted average of its current Level 2 hashtag children after each new insertion.

The two-stage hashtag-based retrieval has been implemented: for apprentice VLM queries, first retrieve all cache entries sharing the same Level 1 hashtag as the query, then perform top-k dense retrieval within that filtered set.
The MMMU dataset loading pipeline has been implemented to filter out instances with multiple images, correctly yielding 146 dev, 857 val, and 9702 test instances.
The VL-ICL dataset loading pipeline has been implemented for the TextOCR and Clevr subtasks with the correct dev (200) and val (800) split sizes.
The GPT-4o master VLM MMMU prompt has been implemented per Appendix A, instructing the model to include reasoning steps and answer in the format 'ANSWER: LETTER CHOICE'.

The GPT-4o master VLM CLEVR prompt has been implemented per Appendix A: 'How many objects in the image have the <question>? Please include your reasoning steps, then answer in the format ANSWER: NUMBER.'

The GPT-4o master VLM TextOCR prompt has been implemented per Appendix A: 'What text is shown in the red box? Only answer with the largest text. Please include reasoning steps, then answer in format ANSWER: TEXT.'
The OpenFlamingo MMMU in-context learning prompt has been implemented per Appendix A, interleaving support images and QA pairs with <|endofchunk|> tokens and ending with 'The answer is'.
The OpenFlamingo CLEVR in-context learning prompt has been implemented per Appendix A: '<image> <shot-1> <|endofchunk|> ... <image> How many <question> objects? The answer is'.

The OpenFlamingo TextOCR in-context learning prompt has been implemented per Appendix A: '<image> <shot-1> <|endofchunk|> ... <image> What text is shown in the red box? Only answer with the largest text. The answer is'.

The Qwen MMMU in-context learning prompt has been implemented per Appendix A, including a system message, n in-context examples provided as 'Human: <question> <option> ... Assistant: <gpt4-o response>', and instructions to format the answer as 'ANSWER: <LETTER CHOICE>'.
The Qwen MMMU auxiliary VLM prompt for generating apprentice responses (used for retrieval enhancement) has been implemented per Appendix A without in-context examples.

The apprentice VLM inference with in-context examples has been implemented as a single forward pass: prepend retrieved QA pairs to the inference prompt without any weight updates to the apprentice VLM.

The rule-based answer parser has been implemented to extract the final letter choice from VLM responses for MMMU (multiple-choice) and to check for exact label match for VL-ICL (open response), returning None when parsing fails.
The accuracy evaluation metric has been implemented, correctly ignoring None parsed responses rather than counting them as random choices.

The static cache evaluation configuration has been implemented: cache is pre-constructed from offline data (val or dev split), remains fixed during evaluation, and only the apprentice VLM is used for query serving.

The dynamic cache evaluation configuration has been implemented: queries are processed as a data stream, cache grows via master VLM insertions, and the multiplexer randomly selects between master and apprentice VLMs at a specified ratio.
Half-cache-size experiments have been implemented by restricting the cache to 50% of its original capacity to evaluate the effect of cache size on in-context learning performance.

The static performance experiments with Qwen-7B on MMMU have been executed successfully for all retrieval strategies: no in-context, hierarchical cluster-based, and multiple dense retrieval variants (varying cache embedding, query embedding, and cache size).

The static performance experiments with OpenFlamingo-3B and OpenFlamingo-9B on MMMU and VL-ICL (CLEVR, TextOCR) have been executed successfully for both Val-Test-Dev and Dev-Test-Val caching strategies, with 0-shot, 1-shot, and 2-shot settings.

The dynamic performance experiments on MMMU with Qwen-7B have been executed successfully across all cache initialization methods (NoStart, ColdStart, WarmStart, Hierarchical) and all apprentice VLM usage levels (10%, 30%, 50%, 70%, 90%).

The ablation study on hierarchical retrieval has been executed successfully, varying the number of clusters K on the MMMU dataset to analyze the sensitivity of hierarchical retrieval to this hyperparameter.

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 1: without in-context learning, Qwen-7B achieves approximately 24.66% (Val-Test-Dev) and 34.42% (Dev-Test-Val); hierarchical retrieval reaches approximately 39.04% and 39.44% respectively.

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 1: the best dense retrieval strategy uses cache:image embeddings and query:image+text embeddings from extracted keywords, achieving ~35.62% (Val-Test-Dev) and ~39.20% (Dev-Test-Val).
The execution outcomes match the results in Table 1: restricting cache size to half reduces dense retrieval performance (e.g., ~34.25% vs ~35.62% for the best strategy in Val-Test-Dev setting).

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 2 (Val-Test-Dev): OpenFlamingo-3B achieves 15.75% at 0-shot on MMMU and improves to ~17.12% with 1-shot using full cache; CLEVR improves from 7.50% (0-shot) to 20.50% (1-shot) and 28.00% (2-shot).
The execution outcomes match the results in Table 2: OpenFlamingo-9B achieves 20.55% at 0-shot and improves to 25.34% with 1-shot on MMMU using full cache.

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 3 (Dev-Test-Val): OpenFlamingo-3B achieves 22.75% at 0-shot and improves to 23.92% with 1-shot on MMMU; OpenFlamingo-9B improves from 24.97% (0-shot) to 26.25% (1-shot).

The execution outcomes match Figure 5: in the WarmStart dynamic setting, the MMMU score gain with CoT grows from 0.007 (7%) to 0.77 (7.7%) as apprentice VLM usage increases from 10% to 90%, validating the up-to-7.7% overall improvement claim.
The execution outcomes match Figure 5: the hierarchical retrieval method underperforms dense retrieval in the dynamic setting due to fixed cluster hyperparameters while cache content evolves.

The execution outcomes match Figure 6: when apprentice VLM usage is between 30% and 70%, CoT achieves 10%-23% performance gains; WarmStart at 10% usage achieves up to 38% performance gain, validating the up-to-36.6% apprentice boost claim.

The execution outcomes match Figure 7: at low apprentice VLM usage rates (10%-30%), the MMMU score increases between the first and third temporal dataset splits, demonstrating CoT's self-improvement as the cache grows over time.

The execution outcomes match Figure 4: hierarchical retrieval performance is sensitive to the number of clusters K, and there exists an optimal K value; performance degrades when K is too small or too large.
